Name: rate_distortion_neural_compression
Title: Information-Theoretic Limits of Neural Network Compression: A Rate-Distortion Approach
Short Hypothesis: Neural network compression can be formulated as a rate-distortion problem, where the goal is to minimize the number of bits required to represent the network while maintaining a certain level of predictive performance. We hypothesize that the fundamental limit of compression is bounded by the mutual information between the network's weights and the task's data distribution.
Related Work: Recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation have explored training-free methods for precision allocation and signal separation in neural networks. However, these approaches do not provide a fundamental limit on the compressibility of neural networks. Our work builds upon the concept of rate-distortion theory and mutual information to establish a theoretical bound on the compression of neural networks.
Abstract: We propose a novel approach to neural network compression by formulating it as a rate-distortion problem. By leveraging the concept of mutual information, we establish a theoretical bound on the compressibility of neural networks. Our approach provides a fundamental limit on the number of bits required to represent a neural network while maintaining a certain level of predictive performance. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through experiments on various neural network architectures and datasets. Our work provides a new perspective on neural network compression and has implications for the development of more efficient and scalable neural network models.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments on various neural network architectures and datasets to evaluate the effectiveness of our approach. We will use metrics such as bits-per-weight and predictive performance to measure the compression efficiency and accuracy of our approach. We will also compare our approach with existing compression methods such as quantization and pruning to demonstrate its advantages.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk of our approach is that it may not be applicable to all types of neural networks or datasets. Additionally, the computation of mutual information can be challenging, and approximations may be required. Furthermore, our approach may require significant computational resources to train and evaluate the neural networks.
